critic_raw:
The RNN is described as capturing 'temporal dependencies in treatment sequences,' but the features fed to the model are 'patient demographics, medical history, and treatment details' — all of which are static at discharge time. If the RNN receives a fixed vector of pre-discharge features rather than a genuine time-stamped sequence, there are no temporal dependencies to model and the RNN becomes a feedforward network with unnecessary recurrent architecture. The core advantage claimed for the RNN is never actually exercised if the input is not sequential.

The stratified random split is described as appropriate because 'short-term temporal autocorrelations in patient readmission outcomes are sufficiently weak.' Over five years, clinical practice at a single hospital will change substantially — new clinical guidelines, formulary changes, staffing changes, and population shifts. The model trained on the first three years evaluated on randomly mixed data from year five will see the same temporal distribution in both train and test, masking any temporal generalization failure that would occur in actual deployment.

AUROC is the primary metric, which is appropriate for discrimination. However, for 30-day readmission prediction specifically, the clinically and operationally standard benchmark is comparison against the LACE index (length of stay, acuity, comorbidities, emergency visits). Using logistic regression as the only baseline rather than a validated clinical scoring tool means the experiment does not establish whether the RNN beats the actual state of clinical practice.

Confounding is addressed through 'comprehensive feature inclusion, particularly addressing socio-economic disparities.' Adding socio-economic features as model inputs does not control for confounding — it conditions on these variables, which is appropriate for prediction but does not allow causal attribution of the RNN's performance advantage to its architectural properties rather than to the richer feature set.
all_issues_raised: The RNN receives static pre-discharge features rather than a time-stamped sequence, so there are no temporal dependencies to model — the RNN's claimed architectural advantage cannot be exercised or verified with the described input format.

A stratified random split over five years of hospital data ensures the model is evaluated on the same temporal distribution it was trained on, masking potential concept drift from clinical guideline changes that would cause deployment failures.

The only baseline is logistic regression; the clinical state of practice for 30-day readmission uses validated scoring tools like LACE index, and the experiment does not establish whether the RNN surpasses the actual clinical benchmark.

Including socio-economic features as model inputs conditions on potential confounders for prediction purposes but does not control for them in the causal sense — performance differences between RNN and logistic regression could be attributed to feature access rather than architecture.
all_issues_adjudicated: The RNN receives static pre-discharge features rather than a time-stamped sequence, so there are no temporal dependencies to model — the RNN's claimed architectural advantage cannot be exercised or verified with the described input format.

A stratified random split over five years of hospital data ensures the model is evaluated on the same temporal distribution it was trained on, masking potential concept drift from clinical guideline changes that would cause deployment failures.

The only baseline is logistic regression; the clinical state of practice for 30-day readmission uses validated scoring tools like LACE index, and the experiment does not establish whether the RNN surpasses the actual clinical benchmark.

Including socio-economic features as model inputs conditions on potential confounders for prediction purposes but does not control for them in the causal sense — performance differences between RNN and logistic regression could be attributed to feature access rather than architecture.
